event: no history of loss of consciousness
no change in stool habits/urine color
no history of recent trauma or falls
reported to have previous uncomplicated malarial infections
pulse rate of 103 beats/min in sinus rhythm
rest of serum electrolytes, liver, and coagulation profile were normal
high parasitemia with more than 1000 trophozoites/200 WBC, HPF
admitted to the intensive care unit (ICU)
commenced on an intravenous artesunate-based regimen
marked improvement in both clinical status and lab parameters
shifted to the general ward after 3 days of ICU stay
acutely developed features of acute abdomen
progressive, dull, constant generalized abdominal pain
more so on the left upper quadrant
CBC revealed a low Hb of 7.1 g/dL
Ultrasound showed hypoechoic nodular cystic area
CT scan revealed hyperdense intrasplenic hematoma
demonstrated splenic laceration on the superolateral aspect
findings were in line with grade 3 splenic injury
attributed to the consequence of severe malaria
no evidence of other documented etiologies such as trauma
2.5 L of frank blood without purulence was evacuated
the spleen was enlarged and very firm
with a long laceration on the upper pole
extending laterally involving the fine covering capsule
and not much of the splenic parenchyma
scheduled clinic visits for post-splenectomy care
MSR can occur acutely any time during the course of illness
MSR generally presents as circulatory shock
with peritonitis due to intra-abdominal bleeding
high index of clinical suspicion is warranted
who presents with signs of acute abdomen
splenectomy being indicated or reserved for hemodynamically unstable patients
